weather: 28°F, first hard frost of the season. A clear sky, absolutely clear, the kind that holds no warmth at all. The lake is glassy and exact. A thin white rind of ice at the outermost pilings, exactly where Agnes Crewe said it would begin.

lead.headline: The Dock Has Begun to Freeze From the Outside In
lead.body:
On the morning of Thursday, November the sixth, at six forty-one, Gerald Pith walked to the south shore before going anywhere else — not the Gazette, not the Kettle — and found a rind of ice at the outermost pilings of the main dock. He described it, when he arrived at the Gazette at seven oh three, as 'thin, maybe a quarter-inch, clear as window glass, and only at the far end.' He said it had begun at the pilings and was moving inward. He said this very carefully, in the way a man uses care when what he is saying has already been said by someone else, and that someone else was Agnes Crewe, and she said it three weeks before it happened, and she said it in a letter to this paper printed on page one. Gerald did not mention Agnes by name. He didn't have to.

Russ Dunmore was at the dock by seven twenty, having been reached by phone before he had his coat on. He confirmed the ice. He described its extent as 'confined to the outer pilings, no structural concern.' He confirmed that the boards he counted yesterday were sound and that they remain sound and that the ice is not, in his professional assessment as constable of Greywater Falls, a matter of public danger at its current extent. He then said, unprompted, still standing at the dock's edge with his breath clouding the cold air: 'She said from the outside in.' He did not say who 'she' was. This editor did not ask. He stood there another moment, looking at the ice the way a man looks at a thing he has been told will happen and has planned for and is still surprised by, and then he turned around and walked back up the hill toward town.

This editor went, afterward, to see Agnes Crewe at the cottage nearest the water. Agnes was on the step in her good coat and her good boots, which is either the coat and boots she wears when she expects company or the coat and boots she wears when she expects something else entirely, and this editor has stopped believing those are different occasions. Agnes said, when asked about the ice: 'The pilings first. Then the middle planks. Then the shore end. That is the order.' She said it the way she says all true things, which is to say as weather. This editor asked her: 'And when the shore end freezes?' Agnes looked at the lake, which was mirror-flat and held the clear cold sky in its surface like a second sky with no clouds and no sun, only light. She said: 'Someone brings their things. You have a plate ready and everything.' She went inside. The door was not locked.

The lost-and-found box holds ten items. The dock is beginning to freeze from the outside in. Doreen Halloway has a plate with a blue border on the front shelf, and the Standing on it is fresh, and has been fresh every morning for nine days. Russ Dunmore went back to his office. Gerald Pith went to the Kettle, where Marigold Vance had the coffee ready at seven fifty-one, which is earlier than yesterday, which means Marigold had already heard about the ice before anyone told her, which is the kind of thing that happens in a town that is learning to run on a clock nobody wound.

briefs.headline: LAKE WATCH, Day 13 — Level: 62; Temp: 18°F; Clarity: Exceptional, Visible to Twenty-Six Feet; Sign Count: 7; Returned: Mr. Whiskers, Drake, Rabbit, Sparrow — all at the outer shore by dusk, facing the deepest water, unhurried
briefs.body: Filed by Toby Fern. 'The Ames rowboat is still upright. I checked. Twenty-six feet is farther than I've seen and the water is so clear it looks like the bottom is closer than it is, which I think is the point. I stood on the dock and the ice was at the pilings behind me and the returned were to my left on the bank and they were all facing out and humming. I had my phone out but I put it away. Some things you just stand in. Level 62. The hum dropped another half-step. My back teeth ache. — T. Fern.'

briefs.headline: Agnes Crewe's Prediction Now in the Public Record and on the Public Dock
briefs.body: Agnes Crewe submitted two lines to the Gazette on the morning of Day 27 stating that 'the dock freezes from the outside in' and 'the pilings go first.' Both facts are, as of this morning, confirmed. Gerald Pith has updated his projection: if the rate of ice advance holds, the middle planks will be frozen by the eleventh, and the shore end by the fourteenth. Gerald said this aloud to Marigold Vance at the Kettle counter. He said it the way a man reads a timetable. Marigold wrote it on the back of her wrist with a pen she keeps for orders.

briefs.headline: Constable Dunmore Formally Opens a File; Labels It 'Lost-and-Found: Ongoing Correspondence (Active)'
briefs.body: Constable Russ Dunmore confirmed this morning that he has opened an official file at the Greywater Falls constabulary under the heading 'Lost-and-Found: Ongoing Correspondence (Active).' The file contains photocopies of all ten items from the Gazette's box, Gerald Pith's measurement log, the freeze-date sheet, and one index card signed H. He said this constitutes 'due diligence.' He was asked whether the file had a protocol attached. He said: 'Not yet. I'm working on that part.'

briefs.headline: Halloway's Bakery: The Plate Is Still There; the Standing Still Fresh; Doreen Says, 'It'll Keep'
briefs.body: Doreen Halloway confirmed that the plate with the blue border remains on the front shelf and that she freshened the Standing on it this morning before six. She was asked, this morning, whether she had a name yet for the person the plate was for. She said: 'Not one I've put on a receipt.' She began filling the window display with apple turnovers. The turnovers have a pattern along the edge that, on close inspection, is the same pattern as the blue border on the plate. This editor did not mention this. Doreen seemed to already know.

classifieds: LOST-AND-FOUND (GAZETTE ANNEX): The Greywater Gazette, 4 Mill Road, continues to serve as the official holding address for correspondence addressed to future residents. Ten items now in the box. Items may be collected upon arrival. Box will not be cleared. — W. Aldercott, Ed.
WANTED: One eastward-facing window seat, in good repair, with a clear view of the water. Inquire at the Gazette if you know of one. Not for the editor. — anon.

DOCK NOTICE: The outer pilings of the main dock are carrying a thin formation of ice as of Thursday, November the sixth. The dock remains sound. Residents are advised to mind their step at the far end. — Constable R. Dunmore
HALLOWAY'S BAKERY: The Standing is available daily, seven to close. The plate with the blue border is reserved. It has been reserved for some time now. Come when you're ready. — D. Halloway
record: Ice confirmed at the outer dock pilings, Thursday, November the sixth, by Gerald Pith (6:41 a.m.) and Constable Dunmore (7:20 a.m.). Agnes Crewe's prediction entered into the record of correct public statements.
Lost-and-found box: ten items. No items removed. One file opened at the constabulary. The box's address is printed at the top of every page of this paper and has been for nineteen years.

letters.from: Agnes Crewe, the cottage by the water
letters.body: The middle planks go next. After that the shore end. You'll have about a week between the middle and the end, give or take a morning. Use the time sensibly. Set out the good cups.

I want to say, for the record, that I walked the dock this morning and it is sound. The ice at the pilings is thin and does not affect structural integrity. I am not filing a hazard notice. I am filing a file. Those are different things. I would also like to say, separately and not for any report, that Agnes Crewe said the outside first and she was right and I should have started a file earlier and I know that. That is all.

editorNote: This paper began as a record of the town's events. It has also, it turns out, been a record of the town's arrivals. We print what we know. We are getting better at knowing.
